content.text: is a system that decouples the act of

. When your web application receives a user action that requires significant computation — sending a batch of emails, generating a PDF report, calling a third-party API, or running an ML inference — the request handler places a serialized description of that work (a
) onto a queue and returns immediately. One or more independent
consume tasks from the queue and execute them, entirely outside the HTTP request-response cycle.
is the dominant Python task queue library. It defines a decorator-based API for marking Python functions as tasks, manages serialization of task messages, connects to a pluggable
(Redis and RabbitMQ are the two most-used options), and provides workers that pick up those messages and execute the corresponding functions. Celery also ships

, a scheduler that injects periodic tasks into the broker at configurable intervals. In the agentic AI context, Celery is the practical tool that lets you run LLM inference calls, embedding generation, retrieval pipelines, and multi-step agent workflows without blocking user-facing endpoints or hitting HTTP timeouts.

The library was created by Ask Solem in 2009 and has become a foundational component of the Python web ecosystem. Production deployments process millions of tasks per day — Disqus, Instagram, and Mozilla are among the well-documented examples. It integrates natively with Django via
, and can be dropped into any Python application with minimal ceremony.

Without a task queue, every piece of slow work must happen synchronously inside a web request handler. A route that triggers a 10-second LLM completion will hold an HTTP connection open for 10 seconds, consuming a precious worker thread or process slot. Under moderate concurrency this exhausts your server's capacity quickly; under heavy load it causes cascading timeouts and user-visible errors. The problem is structural, not fixable with faster hardware alone.
The concrete pain points task queues solve are:
(typically 30-120 s, imposed by load balancers and CDNs);
such as email delivery, file uploads to S3, or REST API calls that take several seconds;
where a sudden spike of requests must not overwhelm downstream services; and

such as nightly report generation, cache warming, or token expiry cleanup. For agentic AI systems, the stakes are especially high: a single Claude API call can take 5-60 seconds for long documents, so every AI-assisted action in a user-facing product essentially requires a task queue to remain practical.
— The web process enqueues a task and returns a task ID in milliseconds. The user gets a 202 Accepted response and can poll or subscribe to progress updates.
— Workers are stateless consumers. Adding more workers means starting more processes, on the same machine or across a fleet. Throughput scales nearly linearly up to broker limits.
— A crashing worker does not affect the web server or the broker. Tasks remain in the queue and are reprocessed by another worker.
— Celery supports configurable retry policies with exponential backoff, letting transient failures (network blips, rate limits, temporary API errors) heal without manual intervention.
— You can define multiple named queues and route different task types to them, assigning more workers to high-priority queues without code changes.
— Celery Beat eliminates the need for cron jobs or external schedulers. Schedules are stored in your database and manageable via the Django admin.
— Celery emits events for every task lifecycle transition. Tools like Flower and Grafana can display real-time worker throughput, failure rates, and queue depths.
— You now manage a broker (Redis or RabbitMQ), worker processes, and optionally Celery Beat as separate infrastructure components. Each is another thing to monitor, deploy, and back up.
— The default acknowledgement mode means a task may execute more than once if a worker crashes mid-execution. All tasks should be designed to be idempotent, which is not always trivial.
— Storing task results in Redis or the database adds write pressure. Many tasks have no return value that callers actually poll; for those, disabling the result backend with

— Asynchronous execution makes tracing errors harder. Stack traces appear in worker logs rather than in the web request trace. A distributed tracing system such as OpenTelemetry with Jaeger is necessary for comfortable debugging at scale.

— Task arguments are serialized to JSON (or pickle, which carries security risks). You cannot pass arbitrary Python objects as arguments; you must pass IDs or serializable primitives and re-fetch the data inside the task.
— For high-throughput, low-latency stream processing, Kafka or Faust is more appropriate. Celery is designed for jobs that run seconds to minutes, not sub-millisecond event processing.

, Celery serializes the task call into a message — a JSON blob containing the task name, positional and keyword arguments, a unique task ID, retry count, queue name, expiry time, and headers for tracing. That message is published to the broker using AMQP (for RabbitMQ) or Redis list primitives.

Workers run an event loop that issues a BRPOP or basic_get against the broker. When a message arrives, the worker deserializes it, looks up the registered task by name, executes the function, and acknowledges the message. The acknowledgement removes the message from the broker. If acknowledgement never arrives because the worker was killed, the broker requeues the message after a visibility timeout.
: Celery forks N worker child processes (defaulting to CPU count). Each child handles one task at a time. For I/O-bound workloads such as LLM API calls, the

pool is far more efficient, allowing thousands of concurrent network requests in a single worker process via cooperative multitasking. For CPU-bound work such as on-device model inference, prefork with one child per CPU core is correct.
The sustained throughput of a Celery deployment is bounded by:
is the number of worker processes or coroutines,
is the concurrency per worker (for gevent pools), and
is the mean task execution time in seconds. For example, 4 workers each with gevent concurrency 100 handling LLM calls averaging 10 s each can sustain up to
— serialized message published to broker queue.
Broker stores the message, persisting it to disk if configured for durability.
Worker fetches the message and marks it as unacknowledged (reserved).
On success: worker stores result in the result backend and acknowledges the message.
On failure: worker schedules a retry with backoff, or moves the message to a Dead Letter Queue if max retries is exceeded.
The diagram below shows all Celery components and the data flows between them, including the normal execution path, the retry and error path, and the Celery Beat periodic scheduler.

attrs.alt: Celery Task Queue End-to-End Architecture: Producer, Message Broker, Workers, Result Backend, Retry Path, Celery Beat Scheduler

content.text: Publishes task messages; your web app or API handler
Django view, FastAPI endpoint, CLI script
Stores and routes task messages; provides queue durability
Consumes messages, executes task functions, stores results
Stores return values and task state for polling
Scheduler that injects periodic tasks into the broker
Celery Beat process with database-backed schedule
Real-time monitoring UI for workers and tasks

. The waiter (your web request handler) writes an order on a ticket and drops it in the kitchen's order wheel (the message broker). The waiter does not stand in the kitchen watching the food cook — they return immediately to the dining room and take more orders. The cooks (workers) pull tickets from the wheel, prepare each dish, and ring the bell when done. The customer (your user) can check back at any time by polling the task ID to see if their dish is ready. If a cook burns a dish (task failure), the ticket goes back on the wheel for another cook to retry.
The key insight the analogy reveals is that the broker is the
: the producer and consumers never talk to each other directly. Either side can fail, scale, or be deployed independently. This is the defining property of any message-queue-based architecture.
Any task that takes more than roughly 1 second and is triggered by a user action — LLM completions, file processing, email sending, PDF generation.
Workflows that must retry on transient failure: third-party API calls, payment webhooks, external data fetches.
Bursty workloads that would overwhelm synchronous handlers, such as processing uploaded files from a batch import.
Agentic pipelines: orchestrating multi-step chains of LLM calls where each step can be retried, monitored, and observed independently.
Periodic maintenance: nightly analytics aggregation, cache warming, token refresh, database cleanup.
Fan-out operations: given one input, spawn N parallel subtasks such as analyzing each section of a document in parallel using a Celery group.
You need sub-100 ms latency and synchronous results. In that case, keep the computation in-process or use asyncio.
Your task volume is very low (a few tasks per hour) and your infrastructure budget is minimal. A simple threading.Thread or a cron job is less overhead.
You need ordered, exactly-once processing of high-volume event streams. Use Kafka or a purpose-built streaming system.
You need long-running stateful workflows with human approval steps. Consider Temporal or Prefect instead.

# Install Celery and the Redis broker/backend driver
pip install celery[redis]

# For Django integration
pip install django-celery-results django-celery-beat

# Optional: monitoring UI
pip install flower
file at the same level as your Django

# myproject/celery.py
import os
from celery import Celery

os.environ.setdefault("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "myproject.settings")

app = Celery("myproject")

# Load configuration from Django settings, namespaced with CELERY_
app.config_from_object("django.conf:settings", namespace="CELERY")

# Auto-discover tasks.py in all INSTALLED_APPS
app.autodiscover_tasks()
Expose the app from your project's

# myproject/__init__.py
from .celery import app as celery_app
__all__ = ("celery_app",)

# myproject/settings.py

CELERY_BROKER_URL = "redis://localhost:6379/0"
CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND = "redis://localhost:6379/1"
CELERY_RESULT_EXPIRES = 86400          # seconds — 24 hours

CELERY_TASK_SERIALIZER = "json"
CELERY_RESULT_SERIALIZER = "json"
CELERY_ACCEPT_CONTENT = ["json"]

CELERY_TIMEZONE = "UTC"
CELERY_ENABLE_UTC = True

# Acknowledge tasks only after completion, not before (safer on crash)
CELERY_TASK_ACKS_LATE = True

# Fetch one task at a time per worker — important for heterogeneous task durations
CELERY_WORKER_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER = 1

# myapp/tasks.py
from celery import shared_task

@shared_task(
    bind=True,
    max_retries=5,
    default_retry_delay=30,
    name="myapp.tasks.send_email_report",
)
def send_email_report(self, user_id: int, report_type: str) -> dict:
    """Generate and email a report. Uses bind=True for self.retry()."""
    try:
        from myapp.models import User
        from myapp.reports import generate_report
        from myapp.email import send_report_email

        user = User.objects.get(pk=user_id)
        report = generate_report(user, report_type)
        send_report_email(user.email, report)

        return {"status": "sent", "user_id": user_id, "report_type": report_type}

    except Exception as exc:
        # Exponential backoff: 30s, 60s, 120s, 240s, 480s
        raise self.retry(exc=exc, countdown=30 * (2 ** self.request.retries))

# Worker with prefork (CPU-bound tasks)
celery -A myproject worker --loglevel=info --concurrency=4

# Worker with gevent (I/O-bound / LLM API calls)
celery -A myproject worker --loglevel=info --pool=gevent --concurrency=50

# Celery Beat (run as a separate process)
celery -A myproject beat --loglevel=info \
    --scheduler django_celery_beat.schedulers:DatabaseScheduler

# Monitoring UI at http://localhost:5555
celery -A myproject flower
This self-contained example needs only Celery and Redis. No Django. It demonstrates basic task dispatch, result fetching, and parallel group execution.

# standalone_example.py
# Prerequisite: docker run -p 6379:6379 redis:alpine
# Terminal 1: celery -A standalone_example worker --loglevel=info
# Terminal 2: python standalone_example.py

from celery import Celery, group

app = Celery(
    "standalone",
    broker="redis://localhost:6379/0",
    backend="redis://localhost:6379/1",
)

@app.task
def add(x: int, y: int) -> int:
    return x + y

@app.task
def multiply(x: float, y: float) -> float:
    return x * y

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Fire-and-forget (no result polling)
    add.delay(4, 6)

    # Synchronous result fetch — fine for scripts, never do this in a web handler
    result = add.apply_async(args=[10, 20])
    print("Task ID:", result.id)
    print("Result:", result.get(timeout=10))  # -> 30

    # Parallel group: run 5 tasks concurrently, collect all results
    job = group(add.s(i, i) for i in range(5))
    group_result = job.apply_async()
    print("Group results:", group_result.get(timeout=30))  # -> [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

    # Chain: output of add feeds as first arg into multiply
    from celery import chain
    pipeline = chain(add.s(3, 4), multiply.s(10))
    print("Chain result:", pipeline.apply_async().get(timeout=10))  # -> 70.0
Example 2 — Async LLM Summarization Task with Progress Tracking
A realistic pattern for an agentic AI application using the Anthropic SDK. The task stores progress metadata so the frontend can display a live status bar.
Real-World Example — Multi-Step Contract Processing Pipeline

A legal-tech SaaS processes uploaded contracts through four pipeline stages: text extraction, clause classification via Claude, risk scoring, and notification delivery. Each stage is a separate Celery task, chained together so each step is independently retryable, observable, and scalable.

At production scale, this organization processes around 500 contracts per hour using 8 worker instances each running gevent with concurrency 20 (160 concurrent coroutines total). A typical 40-page contract completes in under 45 seconds end-to-end. The chain structure ensures that if
is never called and the contract stays in a consistent partially-processed state that can be resumed cleanly on retry.
Passing Non-Serializable Objects as Task Arguments

Passing a Django model instance or any complex Python object as a task argument is one of the most common mistakes. Celery will fail with JSON serialization or, worse, silently encode stale data with pickle.

# WRONG — passing a model instance directly
from myapp.models import User
from myapp.tasks import process_user

user = User.objects.get(pk=1)
process_user.delay(user)   # Fails with JSON, or encodes stale state with pickle

# RIGHT — pass the primary key and re-fetch inside the task
process_user.delay(user_id=1)

# Inside the task:
@shared_task
def process_user(user_id: int):
    user = User.objects.get(pk=user_id)  # fresh, consistent read
    ...
inside a running task waits for another task to complete. If the worker pool is saturated, no other task can start, causing a deadlock.
, a task can run more than once on worker crash. Any task with side effects — sending emails, creating database records, charging payments — must be guarded with an idempotency check.
Forgetting to Set ACKS_LATE and PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER

# settings.py — these two settings should almost always be set together
CELERY_TASK_ACKS_LATE = True            # ack after task completes, not before
CELERY_WORKER_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER = 1   # one task at a time per worker process
. A task should do one thing. Compose complex workflows with Celery chains and groups rather than putting all logic in one large function.
. Serialization is the source of most Celery bugs. Pass primary keys and re-fetch inside the task.
(hard kill). LLM calls can hang indefinitely without a timeout.
Use named queues for priority separation
queue served by dedicated workers so they are never blocked behind slow LLM calls.
Store LLM results immediately after the API call
. Write the response to the database before any secondary processing. A crash between the API call and the write wastes your token budget.
. Return a primary key or short status dict from tasks; store large data such as a generated PDF in S3 and return the reference.
Include task IDs in all log messages
so you can correlate task execution across worker logs, broker events, and APM traces.
Prefer apply_async over delay in production code
CPU-bound: local model inference, image processing
I/O-bound: LLM API calls, HTTP requests, DB queries
Same as gevent; uses eventlet async library
Development and debugging only; no parallelism
For LLM workloads using the Anthropic API, the bottleneck is almost always the API rate limit rather than worker count. The sustainable concurrency is bounded by:
is your Anthropic tokens-per-minute quota and
is the average token count per task. With a 100,000 TPM limit and 2,000 tokens per task on average, the sustainable rate is
tasks per second, meaning roughly 50 tasks per minute. Size your gevent concurrency to match this number.
: a growing queue means consumers cannot keep up. Add workers or reduce the task arrival rate.
: Redis holds all unprocessed messages in RAM. Set
, a slow task can block fast tasks sitting in the worker's prefetch buffer. Set it to 1 for heterogeneous task durations.
: task results accumulate over time. Set
to an appropriate TTL and run periodic result cleanup.
Worker restarts due to memory leak
: long-running prefork workers can leak memory from third-party libraries. Use
to recycle worker processes after N tasks.
— Real-time Celery monitoring web UI. Shows active workers, task throughput, success and failure rates, and lets you revoke pending tasks. Start with
— Stores task results in your Django database, enabling task history queries via the ORM and Django admin visibility.
— Stores periodic task schedules in the database, manageable via Django admin without restarting the Beat process.
— Auto-instruments task spans with OpenTelemetry, linking them to the parent HTTP request trace for distributed tracing.
— The messaging library underlying Celery. Understanding it helps when debugging broker connection issues; you rarely use it directly.
— High-availability broker configurations for production. Celery supports both via the
— Exposes Celery metrics (queue depths, task rates, worker counts) as Prometheus metrics for Grafana dashboards.
— A newer Django-based monitoring dashboard with better Django admin integration than Flower. Useful for teams already invested in the Django admin UI.
General async tasks, periodic jobs, Django integration
Simple async tasks, Redis-only, smaller teams
Lightweight tasks, SQLite or Redis broker
asyncio-native tasks with Redis, modern API
For most Python web applications, the choice is between Celery and RQ.
is simpler to set up and sufficient for low-to-medium task volumes with Redis already in the stack. Choose

when you need multiple queues, complex routing, Celery Beat scheduling, RabbitMQ support, or deep Django integration. For AI agent workflows requiring durable state, retries across process restarts, and long-running sub-workflows spanning hours, investigate

Celery's historical default serializer is pickle, which allows arbitrary code execution when deserializing messages. If an attacker can write to your broker, they can execute arbitrary Python on your workers. Always enforce JSON serialization in production settings.

# settings.py — mandatory for production
CELERY_TASK_SERIALIZER = "json"
CELERY_RESULT_SERIALIZER = "json"
CELERY_ACCEPT_CONTENT = ["json"]  # reject pickle even if sent
Never expose Redis or RabbitMQ ports to the public internet. Bind to localhost or a private VPC subnet.
Require authentication. Use a Redis password (
in redis.conf) and include it in the broker URL:
For RabbitMQ, create a dedicated vhost and user with minimal permissions. Do not use the default guest account.
Encrypt broker traffic with TLS. Use the
URL scheme for Redis over TLS when the broker is on a separate host.
: configure tasks to move to a DLQ after max_retries rather than being silently discarded. Review and replay DLQ messages manually.
: for tasks with side effects, store a unique key in Redis or the database before execution and skip if the key already exists.
on time-sensitive tasks such as OTP emails so they are discarded rather than executed hours later from a backed-up queue.

: store Anthropic API keys in environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault), never in Django settings files or source code. Rotate without worker restarts via environment injection at deploy time.
and retry with a long countdown (60-300 s). Use a shared Redis counter if multiple workers share one API key to prevent thundering herd retries.

# Idempotent, rate-limit-safe LLM task
import redis
import anthropic
from celery import shared_task

_redis = redis.Redis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/2")

@shared_task(bind=True, max_retries=5, name="ai.tasks.safe_llm_call")
def safe_llm_call(self, prompt: str, idempotency_key: str) -> str:
    """
    Idempotent LLM call with rate-limit retry and result caching.
    idempotency_key: stable string unique to this logical request
    (e.g. sha256 of user_id + document_id + prompt fingerprint).
    """
    cache_key = f"llm_result:{idempotency_key}"
    cached = _redis.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return cached.decode()

    try:
        client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        message = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        result = message.content[0].text
        _redis.setex(cache_key, 86400, result)  # cache 24 h to prevent duplicates
        return result

    except anthropic.RateLimitError as exc:
        # Exponential backoff: 60, 120, 240, 480, 960 seconds
        raise self.retry(exc=exc, countdown=60 * (2 ** self.request.retries))
    except anthropic.APIStatusError as exc:
        if exc.status_code >= 500:
            raise self.retry(exc=exc, countdown=30)
        raise  # 4xx errors are not retriable
Task queues decouple work from requests
: the web handler publishes a message and returns in milliseconds; workers execute the actual work asynchronously and independently.
: your application code (producer), a message broker (Redis or RabbitMQ), and one or more worker processes. The result backend is optional but necessary if callers poll for results.
Use gevent workers for LLM workloads
: Anthropic API calls are I/O-bound and take 5-60 seconds. Gevent allows hundreds of concurrent API calls in a single worker process, far outperforming prefork for this pattern.
. Serialization is the root cause of most Celery bugs. Pass primary keys and re-fetch data inside the task with a fresh database read.
. With acks_late enabled, a task may run more than once. Guard all side effects with idempotency keys or database flags.
for heterogeneous or long-running tasks to prevent slow tasks from blocking fast ones that sit pre-fetched in a worker buffer.
Handle rate limits with exponential backoff
and retry with a countdown of 60 seconds or more, scaling exponentially with the retry count.
. Set JSON serialization in settings and accept only JSON content. Pickle enables remote code execution from a compromised broker.
Celery is the right default for Python async tasks
, but consider RQ for simpler cases, Temporal for long-running durable workflows, and Kafka for high-throughput stream processing.
